old_claim.note: Invention Privilege Systems is an earlier historical predecessor or foundation, not a one-to-one engineering dependency.

new_claim.note: Professional patent examination is an administrative layer inside a statutory patent-granting system; the 1836 Act created the Patent Office and required examination before grant.

source_shape.source_locator: USPTO 1836 milestone says the Patent Act required examination before patents were granted and established professional patent examiners.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The 1836 professional examiner system was part of a statutory Patent Office grant process.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports replacing a broad invention-privilege predecessor with the narrower patent-system prerequisite because professional examination only functions as a grant step inside a statutory patent regime.

ontology_before: Technical Patent Examination depended on a broad early invention-privilege node with only historical-predecessor force.
ontology_after: Professional Patent Examination depends on the statutory patent-system node as the required legal container for examination before grant.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: technical_patent_examination now depends on patent_systems and no longer directly on early_patent_systems.
would_reject_if: The node were rescoped to premodern discretionary invention privileges rather than professional patent-office examination.
A stronger source showed professional examination can exist outside a patent-granting legal system.
The patent_systems node were narrowed away from statutory patent grants.
short_reason: Professional examination is a statutory patent-system function, not merely a generic invention-privilege lineage.
